aliases.name: El Vampiro de Moca
aliases.meaning: 'The vampire of Moca'; 1970s livestock-killing precursor to the modern Chupacabra

description.summary: A modern livestock-predator cryptid first widely reported in Puerto Rico in 1995. Two distinct forms dominate: an upright, spined, alien-like Puerto Rican type and a hairless, dog-like type reported in Mexico and the southwestern United States.
description.full: The Chupacabra ('goat-sucker') is among the youngest internationally recognised cryptids. The phenomenon erupted in Puerto Rico in 1995, when a wave of livestock killings — primarily goats, sheep, and chickens, found with puncture wounds and reportedly drained of blood — was attributed to an unknown creature. Witness Madelyne Tolentino's August 1995 description, given to journalists in Canóvanas, fixed the popular image of an upright, bipedal, spined creature with large red eyes. From the late 1990s the Chupacabra label spread across Latin America and into the United States, where reports increasingly described a different animal: a hairless, dog-like quadruped. Necropsies of several alleged southwestern-US Chupacabra carcasses, including the 2007 Cuero, Texas specimen recovered by Phylis Canion, identified them as canids — coyotes, coyote-dog hybrids, or grey foxes — affected by Sarcoptes scabiei (sarcoptic mange). Folklorist and investigator Benjamin Radford spent five years researching the phenomenon and concluded in his 2011 book Tracking the Chupacabra that Tolentino's foundational 1995 description was substantively shaped by the alien creature 'Sil' from the science-fiction film Species (1995), which she had seen shortly before her sighting. The Chupacabra is now treated by mainstream folklorists as a 20th-century legend whose physical-evidence cases reduce to mange-affected canids and other known animals.

etymology.origin: Spanish 'chupacabras', a compound of chupa ('sucks') and cabras ('goats'), coined in 1995 by Puerto Rican comedian and broadcaster Silverio Pérez during radio coverage of the Canóvanas livestock killings.
etymology.first_use: 1995 (Puerto Rico, Spanish; English use within months)

related_phenomena.name: Moca Vampire
related_phenomena.description: 1975 Puerto Rican livestock-killing wave, retrospectively considered a Chupacabra precursor

related_phenomena.name: Jersey Devil
related_phenomena.description: American bipedal cryptid blamed for livestock attacks

related_phenomena.name: Elmendorf Beast
related_phenomena.description: 2004 Texas hairless canid identified as a mange-affected coyote, often called a Chupacabra

features.description: Puerto Rican accounts describe a row of sharp spines or quills running from the neck down the spine
Frequently reported as oversized and red
Two prominent canine teeth used for puncturing prey
Texan/Mexican type largely or entirely hairless, with thickened skin
Original 1995 reports describe a bipedal kangaroo-like stance
Later US and Mexican reports describe a hairless coyote- or dog-like quadruped

physical.morphology: The Chupacabra is unique among major cryptids in having two strongly distinct morphologies: the original 1995 Puerto Rican creature (upright, spined, alien-like) and the later Texan/Mexican type (a hairless canid). Necropsies of carcasses in the second category have repeatedly identified them as coyotes, coyote-dog hybrids, or grey foxes affected by sarcoptic mange.

abilities.description: Said to drain victims' blood through puncture wounds rather than feeding on flesh
Puerto Rican type reported to leap great distances on powerful hind legs
Said to enter pens unseen and to avoid dogs and humans during attacks

behavior.diet: Reported to attack and exsanguinate small livestock — goats, sheep, chickens, rabbits, and occasionally cattle and horses
patterns: Reports cluster around episodic livestock-killing waves rather than continuous activity
Reports follow media coverage of earlier waves (cultural transmission)
Texan/Mexican carcasses repeatedly identified as mange-affected canids

range.description: Reports originated in Puerto Rico in 1995 and within a few years spread across Latin America and into the southwestern and southern United States. Sightings have been reported in Mexico, Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, Chile, Colombia, the Dominican Republic, Nicaragua, Panama, Peru, Texas, New Mexico, Arizona, Maine, and Russia (rare).

hotspots.name: Canóvanas, Puerto Rico

location.description: Town of Canóvanas in eastern Puerto Rico

hotspots.notes: Origin of the modern Chupacabra phenomenon; site of the Tolentino sighting and the 1995 livestock-killing wave

hotspots.name: Cuero, Texas

location.description: DeWitt County town in south Texas

hotspots.notes: Phylis Canion's 2007 hairless-canid carcass; DNA work published 2010 identified the animal as a coyote / coyote-Mexican wolf hybrid

hotspots.name: Elmendorf, Texas

location.description: Bexar County town in south Texas

hotspots.notes: The 'Elmendorf Beast', a hairless canid shot by Devin McAnally; identified by Texas Parks and Wildlife as a mange-affected coyote

first_recorded.source: Puerto Rican press coverage of the early 1995 livestock-killing wave

peak_activity_periods.description: Puerto Rican origin wave; spread through Spanish-language media across Latin America
Texan and Mexican 'hairless canid' wave, with multiple recovered carcasses

temporal.seasonal_patterns: Reports tend to cluster after dry seasons, when canids in marginal condition (and mange-affected animals) are more visible near human settlements.

origins.cultural_roots: The Chupacabra is a self-consciously modern cryptid: it has no clear pre-1995 folklore baseline in Puerto Rico, although the 1975 'Vampiro de Moca' livestock killings are sometimes cited retrospectively as a precursor.
origins.earliest_accounts: Cluster of livestock killings reported in Puerto Rico in March 1995, attributed initially to a 'vampire' figure and from August 1995 to a creature described by Madelyne Tolentino in Canóvanas.
origins.folklore_connections: Connected to wider Latin American legends of livestock-killing supernatural beings, but the modern Chupacabra is a media-era construction whose dissemination tracks 24-hour Spanish-language television and the early Internet.

timeline.event: 'Vampiro de Moca' livestock killings reported around Moca, Puerto Rico
timeline.significance: Sometimes cited retrospectively as a Chupacabra precursor

timeline.event: Wave of livestock killings begins in Puerto Rico, with goats and sheep found drained of blood through small puncture wounds
timeline.significance: Onset of the modern Chupacabra phenomenon
timeline.source: El Nuevo Día and other Puerto Rican press coverage

timeline.event: Madelyne Tolentino reports the foundational sighting in Canóvanas; the term 'chupacabras' is coined on Puerto Rican radio by Silverio Pérez
timeline.significance: Establishes the alien-like Puerto Rican type
timeline.source: Puerto Rican radio and press, August 1995

timeline.event: Reports spread through Mexico and into the United States via Spanish-language media
The 'Elmendorf Beast', a hairless canid, is shot near Elmendorf, Texas by Devin McAnally and identified as a mange-affected coyote
timeline.significance: First widely-publicised carcass of the Texan/Mexican 'hairless canid' type

timeline.event: Phylis Canion of Cuero, Texas, recovers the carcass of a hairless canid near her ranch
timeline.significance: Foundational US specimen of the canine Chupacabra wave
timeline.source: Press coverage and subsequent DNA work

timeline.event: Researchers at Texas State University and the University of California, Davis, sequence the Cuero specimen and identify it as a coyote / Mexican wolf hybrid affected by mange
timeline.significance: Major peer-reviewed identification of a Texan Chupacabra carcass
timeline.source: Texas State University / UC Davis DNA work (2010)

timeline.event: Benjamin Radford publishes Tracking the Chupacabra, presenting evidence that Tolentino's 1995 description was substantively shaped by the film Species (1995)
timeline.significance: Major folkloric debunking of the foundational sighting

history.modern_era: Contemporary investigation is dominated by veterinary, parasitological, and genetic identification of recovered carcasses (consistently mange-affected canids in the Texan/Mexican type), and by folklore and media studies of the dissemination of the legend through Spanish- and English-language media. The phenomenon is widely treated as the textbook case of a 20th-century cryptid born from contemporary media.

notable.name: Madelyne Tolentino sighting
notable.description: Tolentino described a bipedal creature standing roughly 1.2 m tall, with large oval black eyes, a row of spines along its back, three-toed feet, and powerful hind legs. The description was published in Puerto Rican press and is the foundational image of the Chupacabra.

factors: Radford (2011) shows substantive overlap with the alien creature 'Sil' in Species (1995), which Tolentino had seen shortly before
credibility.notes: The most-cited single Chupacabra report; the basis for Radford's debunking work.

notable.name: Elmendorf Beast

credibility_factors: Identification by Texas Parks and Wildlife

notable.description: Devin McAnally shot a hairless canid that had been killing chickens on his property near Elmendorf, Texas. The animal's appearance — hairless, blueish skin, large fangs — was widely reported as a Chupacabra. The Texas Parks and Wildlife Department identified it as a coyote affected by sarcoptic mange.

credibility.notes: Foundational case for the 'mange-affected canid' explanation of US Chupacabra reports.

notable.name: Cuero, Texas carcass (Phylis Canion)
notable.description: Phylis Canion recovered the carcass of a hairless, fanged canid outside Cuero after a series of chicken killings. The body was preserved and later sequenced by researchers at Texas State University and the University of California, Davis, who identified it as a coyote with Mexican wolf ancestry.

credibility.notes: Most-cited US Chupacabra case with full genetic identification.

sources: Texas State University / UC Davis DNA work (2010)

physical.name: Elmendorf Beast specimen
physical.description: Hairless canid carcass shot by Devin McAnally near Elmendorf, Texas.
physical.analysis: Identified by Texas Parks and Wildlife Department as a coyote affected by sarcoptic mange.

physical.name: Cuero, Texas Chupacabra specimen
physical.description: Hairless canid carcass recovered by Phylis Canion outside Cuero, Texas, and preserved.
physical.analysis: Sequenced by Texas State University and UC Davis researchers as a coyote / Mexican wolf hybrid affected by mange.

trace.name: Puerto Rico 1995 livestock injuries
trace.description: Goats, sheep, and chickens found dead with small paired puncture wounds in the neck region.
trace.analysis: Veterinary examination at the time attributed many of the killings to feral dogs and other known predators; the 'drained of blood' description has been contested by pathologists.

paranormal.name: Extraterrestrial origin
paranormal.description: Hypothesis, popular in mid-1990s reporting, that the Chupacabra was an alien creature, possibly escaped from a covert US research site in Puerto Rico (often associated with the El Yunque rainforest).
evidence_cited: Tolentino's description of large dark eyes and an alien gait
Coincidence of sightings with reported UFO activity

scientific.name: Undescribed Caribbean predator
scientific.description: Early hypothesis (largely abandoned) that the 1995 wave was caused by an unknown predator endemic to Puerto Rico.
evidence_cited: Small island fauna with documented endemics

skeptical.name: Mange-affected canids (Texan/Mexican type)
skeptical.description: The hairless 'Chupacabra' carcasses recovered in Texas, Mexico, and elsewhere are coyotes, coyote-dog hybrids, or grey foxes severely affected by Sarcoptes scabiei (sarcoptic mange), which produces hair loss, thickened skin, and emaciation.
proponents: Texas State University / UC Davis researchers
evidence_cited: Necropsy and DNA results from Elmendorf (2004), Cuero (2007), and other carcasses
Known parasitology of S. scabiei in canids

skeptical.name: Cinematic source for the Puerto Rican type
skeptical.description: Tolentino's 1995 foundational description is substantively shaped by the alien creature 'Sil' from the science-fiction film Species (1995), which she had seen shortly before her sighting.
evidence_cited: Timeline of Species's release vs. the Tolentino sighting

skeptical.name: Feral dogs and known predators
skeptical.description: The 1995 livestock-killing wave in Puerto Rico is attributable to feral dogs, mongooses, and other known predators, with deaths consistent with predator throat punctures rather than exsanguination.
proponents: Puerto Rican Department of Agriculture veterinarians
evidence_cited: Absence of measurable systemic blood loss in autopsies

stories: Mexican northern-border ranch attack stories (late 1990s onward)
folklore.beliefs: The Chupacabra is unusual among cryptids in having no deep folklore baseline; it is widely treated by folklorists as a 20th-century legend whose dissemination tracks Spanish-language television and the early Internet.

literature: Tracking the Chupacabra: The Vampire Beast in Fact, Fiction, and Folklore — Benjamin Radford (2011)
Chupacabras and Other Mysteries — Scott Corrales (1997)
film_tv: The X-Files episode 'El Mundo Gira' (1997, season 4)
Scooby-Doo and the Monster of Mexico (2003)
other: Cuero, Texas Chupacabra branding and tourism
Puerto Rican cultural references in music and politics from the late 1990s onwards

researchers.name: Benjamin Radford
researchers.contribution: Five-year folkloric and forensic investigation of the Chupacabra and the foundational Tolentino sighting

researchers.name: Dr. Barry O'Connor
researchers.contribution: Identification of mange-affected canids as the source of US Chupacabra reports

researchers.name: Phylis Canion
researchers.contribution: Recovered and preserved the 2007 Cuero specimen and supported DNA work

researchers.name: Loren Coleman
researchers.contribution: Cryptozoological cataloguing of Chupacabra reports across the Americas

researchers.name: Scott Corrales
researchers.contribution: Early English-language documentation of the Latin American Chupacabra wave

organizations.name: Center for Inquiry
organizations.focus: Folkloric and skeptical investigation of paranormal claims, including Radford's Chupacabra work

organizations.name: Texas Parks and Wildlife Department
organizations.focus: Identification of recovered Texan Chupacabra carcasses

research.ongoing_studies: Active research is concentrated in veterinary parasitology of sarcoptic mange in canids, in folklore studies of contemporary legend and media, and in continuing necropsy work on recovered carcasses across the southwestern United States and northern Mexico.

sources.title: Tracking the Chupacabra: The Vampire Beast in Fact, Fiction, and Folklore
Chupacabra Science: How Evolution Made a Mythical Monster
